metric_value: Aberdeen approached 28 companies; 5 agreed to participate (Dell, IBM, Unisys, Computer Associates, Xerox). Most companies treat launch process as strategic and confidential.
All 5 companies described a global, standardized launch process across hardware/software/services product groups.
2 companies said variations are insignificant; 3 acknowledged individual product groups had some autonomy with meaningful product-to-product variation.

Launch execution always involves a matrix-managed cross-functional team led by a launch team owner, integrating product dev, test, procurement, manufacturing, marketing, sales channel, fulfillment, training, support, finance, AR, PR.
2 of 5 companies had separate launch organizations reporting into VP Marketing (Product and Solutions Launch; Product Release Strategy). 3 had no common launch organization.
Lead time — End of product beta testing: 14 days before launch (Appendix A-1); 15-30 days (A-2). Often data is collected all the way to launch decision.
Product Configurator Ready: requirements 30-45 days; implemented 7-14 days. Order systems require data 45 days in advance; lead time rarely met.
Launch Readiness Review: often starts 90 days before launch, final readiness review 7-14 days prior, senior-management Time-to-Market decision meeting 1-7 days prior.
Pricing (U.S.) signoff: guidelines required 60 days prior; signoff 30-45 days prior. Lead times often not met (may be <30 days).
Product literature print run complete: 14 days (often not until launch date). Catalog placements 30 days. 3-4 weeks out to last-minute.
Press and analyst materials: briefings 45 days prior; materials sent 5 days prior or at launch. Press 2 weeks; analysts 4-6 weeks (separate respondent).
Training Materials: 14 days (one respondent) to 4-6 weeks (another).
Web site: 30 days before launch (multiple respondents agreed).
Sales training: 30 days prior. BOM: 60-90 days prior.

Seven delivery methods used across respondents: (1) hardcopy, (2) Web e-learning, (3) Web site, (4) classroom, (5) electronic documents (.pdf), (6) intranet database, (7) collaboration software. Trend toward webinars.

While a rigorous published framework is defined for the organization, it is rarely executed precisely in an identical fashion — difficult to differentiate between process as defined and as implemented.
As timeframes tighten and pressures for new products increase, there is increasing variation from standard process and increasing autonomy of individual product groups/business units.
In companies without a separate launch org, process-change authority is vague — senior executives of product groups approve changes but no clear owner.
At least one respondent (implied IBM) uses Lotus Notes collaboration software throughout much of the launch process.
Work is done differently across regions; globalization/internationalization typically first North America then rolled out and translated locally; 30 days for sales-literature translation.
